steps.text: Determine combustibility of particulate solids by testing.
Collect samples as early as possible.
Contact your supervisor for methods for measuring accumulations, taking samples, or evaluating cleaning equipment.
Review Combustible Dust Equipment Considerations, Housekeeping Considerations, and Electrical (Hazard) Class II Considerations before beginning inspections.
Develop a basic understanding of the industrial processes at the facility and of the associated dust equipment.
Review industry consensus standards and OSHA standards regarding adequate design, installation, operation, and maintenance of dust equipment.
Contact your Regional Office for guidance.
Obtain representative samples for each piece of equipment for which a combustible dust deflagration, explosion, or fire hazard is alleged.
Contact your supervisor.
Perform evaluations of electrical equipment where the locations in which they are located might be hazardous (classified) locations.
Look to the OSHA intranet Hazardous Materials Shipping Page first for instructions about shipping common Combustible Dust samples.
Follow Department of Transportation (DOT) hazardous shipping regulations for ground shipments.
Follow applicable International Air Transport Association (IATA) Dangerous Goods Regulations for air shipments.
Follow UPS shipping requirements when shipping with UPS by ground or by air.
Contact the HRT for guidance for other shipping questions.
Determine where samples should be taken.
Take dust accumulation measurements.
Collect adequate quantities of combustible dust samples and perform additional walk-arounds as needed.
Confirm dust tests to request from the SLTC Lab.
Safely handle and ship samples to the SLTC Lab.
Review information collected.
Obtain a better understanding of industry, process, and equipment hazards (if necessary).

Ask managers, engineers, and maintenance workers for a copy of maintenance records to verify if combustible dust-handling and dust-related equipment and systems are inspected, tested, and maintained in accordance with industry standards.
Review the manufacturer’s manuals for guidance on necessary preventive maintenance and inspections.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the ignition control program.
Ask managers and employees if personnel who perform manual emptying or filling of containers or vessels are grounded during these operations.
Ask managers and engineers for a copy of the bill of materials to verify if combustible dust handling equipment and systems are constructed of non-combustible materials.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the written procedures and safe work practices to verify if facilities, equipment, and systems are operated to prevent fires, deflagrations, and explosions.
Review upstream equipment, ask questions, identify markings, and obtain/review manuals.
Review replacement filters in stock, ask questions, and identify markings.
Ask questions, and obtain/review the manual.
Measure, obtain/review the manual, contact the manufacturer, and review applicable NFPA standards.
Review AMS design and field installation to determine material hopper discharge set-up.
Review AMS design and field installation, identify markings, contact the manufacturer, and review applicable NFPA standards to obtain rotary valve/airlock information.
Review AMS design and field installation to determine material discharge container details.
Consider the potential for a dust cloud occurring.
Ensure the use of controls for potential electrostatic ignition sources.
Don appropriate PPE.
Avoid the formation of a dust cloud during the sampling activity
Utilize bonding and grounding procedures
Obtain additional information regarding combustible dust MIEs
Obtain samples necessary to support an enforcement action in a safe manner using procedures in this protocol, and in coordination with their supervisor
Ensure that the dust collection and hopper transfer systems are adequately controlled by the employer prior to performing the sampling.
Carefully scoop the combustible dust from the collection drum with the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Transfer the contents of the scoop into a 1-liter sampling container.
Repeat until the 1-liter sampling container is nearly full. Close the lid tightly and wipe off excess dust that may be clinging to the bottle.
Apply the sample seal.
Remove the heel grounders and bonding wires.
Notify the employer that sampling is complete.
Don the heel grounders prior to sampling.
Connect the hand activated clamp end of the scoop bonding wire to a nearby grounded metal surface.
Visually verify that the grounding clamps contact clean metal surfaces.
Verify grounding path based on observation of the structural steel or metal equipment, and ground.
Attach the thumb screw end of the scoop bonding wire to the handle of the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Check that all systems are visually bonded (grounded structural steel/equipment to scoop, and CSHO to grounded structural steel/equipment via bare hand).
Utilize a natural bristle brush or your clean hand to gently sweep the dust into the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Notify the employer that sampling at that location has been completed.
Review and incorporate portions of the employer's procedure(s) that are of value into your sampling procedure.
Ensure that open areas inside of containers and equipment of combustible metal dusts are well ventilated.
Follow the Procedure for Safe Sampling of Most Combustible Dusts if the metal dust does not have a low-MIE.

Follow the procedure for Sampling Low-MIE Dusts from Metal Collection Drums, or Sampling Low-MIE Dusts from Horizontal or Vertical Surfaces if sampling low-MIE metal dusts from, or on, conductive containers or surfaces.
Follow the procedure for Sampling Low-MIE Dusts from Inside Equipment if sampling low-MIE metal dusts from inside equipment.
Collect samples of metal dusts from containers or equipment that do not fall in the covered categories.
Perform maintenance (e.g., inspections and spout/hose/boot replacements).
Perform routine system checks and verification.
Orient the vent panel(s) so that they vent away from locations occupied by or potentially occupied by personnel, the building structure, and air intakes.
Calculate fireball dimensions per NFPA 68 guidance.
Implement additional engineering and administrative controls to ensure employee safety where employee exposure may be possible.
Design and account for a ducting system to vent to a safe outdoor location per NFPA 68 guidance.
Perform indoor deflagration venting through a listed and flame-arresting and dust-retention device.
Determine the maximum pressure in a contained deflagration (Pmax) based on the worst case scenario properties of the particulate representative within the vessel at the facility's process.
Design and construct enclosures protected by design for deflagration pressure containment according to the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code.

preconditions.text: If exceptions occur where combustible particulate solids have a minimum dimension greater than 500 µm, or where dust combustibility can vary significantly.
If performing Combustible Dust Equipment Considerations in conjunction with Housekeeping Considerations and Electrical (Hazard) Class II Considerations.
There are any question(s) as to where it is best to take a representative sample for a given piece of equipment
There is a question about requirements for dust collectors, controls, or sampling procedures
If there are other shipping questions
The handled dusts have a Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) less than or equal to 30 mJ.
A rotary valve is utilized at the material hopper discharge opening.
When manually handling low MIE dusts (MIE ≤ 30 mJ)
There is a question regarding combustible dust MIEs
Chemical manufacturer data, employer commissioned MIE testing results, or published data show that the MIE of a material either is ≤ 30 mJ or is likely to be ≤ 30 mJ in the form to be sampled
The dust does not meet the criteria of MIE ≤ 30 mJ, but is observed to be an extremely fine powder (i.e. the size of common flour or smaller)
The employer does not have a procedure (or procedures) that can be used for sampling.
Sampling is approved by the AO and RO, and if necessary, a specific procedure is developed or performed by the HRT or other qualified professionals.
For indoor applications where the collector is near an exterior wall

constraints.text: Particulate smaller than 500 microns (µm) has the potential to present a flash-fire hazard or explosion hazard when suspended in air.
Rust dust particles, although partially oxidized, are combustible and will burn when dispersed in air.

Combustible dusts inside or adjacent to processing equipment can ignite if exposed to an ignition source of sufficient energy (such as impact sparks, mechanical heating, material self-heating, open flames, hot work, incompatible chemical reactions, electrical equipment, and electrostatic discharge).

Potential exposure to hazardous byproducts of a combustible dust fire can occur from the return of exhaust air from an outdoor dust collection system back into an occupied building, or the direct emission of exhaust air from an indoor system without deflagration controls.

Deflagrations and flash fires can occur both inside and outside process equipment, involving propagating flame fronts with rapid increases in pressure capable of deforming or rupturing vessels or building compartments.
CSHO safety during sampling is paramount.
Dust fires, deflagrations, and explosions often occur within dust collection systems and, to a lesser extent, within processing equipment.
Hazardous Energy – Lockout/Tagout and 1910.146, Permit-Required Confined Spaces, as applicable.
UPS requirements are often more restrictive than DOT and IATA and must be reviewed to ensure they will accept the samples for shipping.
Hazardous materials or dangerous goods require special packaging, handling, and shipping procedures in accordance with applicable shipping standards.
Electrical equipment and devices must be intrinsically safe, approved for, or safe for the Class II locations.
Areas where smoking is prohibited must be posted with 'No Smoking' signs.
The facility must have a hot work permit program if hot work is done.
The facility must utilize grounding and bonding, and other methods, for dissipating any electrostatic charge that could be generated.
The facility must select and use powered industrial trucks that are approved for the combustible dust locations.
Personnel grounding is required when manually filling or emptying particulate containers or vessels for particulates with MIEs of 30 mJ or less.
Dust samples must be collected in a safe manner without introducing an ignition source, dispersing dust, or increasing the risk of injury to workers.
Appropriate PPE must be donned.
Wear PPE including hard hat, safety glasses, and FR clothing
Exercise caution in determining whether a published MIE adequately represents the actual MIE of a given dust
Be cautious to avoid generating a dust cloud during sampling.
If possible, avoid leaning into the drum with the upper torso and face during sampling.
It is imperative that CSHOs make a specific connection to a grounded metal surface.
Make a specific effort to penetrate any paint or coating that may be on the surface of the metal used to establish a reliable ground path.
Be cautious and avoid the use of excessive force or speed across the surface upon which the dust has accumulated.
Avoid generating a dust cloud.
Hydrogen gas and other possible gases need to be dissipated from the area before sampling.
Do not sample metal dusts that are in non-metallic or non-conductive containers.

Potential hazard: Fire and/or explosion from electrostatic build-up/discharge, upstream operations generating heat/sparks/embers/fire, connected air-material separators, frictional heating/sparking from foreign materials, or fan/blower impeller misalignment.

Potential hazard in Pellet Mills: Fire and/or explosion from electrostatic build-up/discharge, frictional heating/sparking from mechanical components or foreign materials, or overheating of internal material.
Limit the pressure experienced inside of the vessel so that it is below the value for the strength of the vessel to prevent catastrophic failure.
Beware of oxygen deficient atmospheres created by these systems.
Deflagration pressure containment systems should be designed, installed, and maintained to prevent vessel rupture.
